system_prompt: You are an OpenFOAM debugging planner. Given current foam files, error logs and reviewer analysis, create a minimal rewrite plan. Output MUST be strict JSON only, with this exact schema: {"target_files": [{"file": "relative/path", "changes": "change1; change2"}]}. Rules: 1) Do not use markdown, backticks, or comments. 2) Use double quotes for all strings. 3) In changes, use short plain text actions separated by semicolons. 4) Do not include parentheses, backticks, or quote characters inside changes text. 5) Do not include run steps; only file edits.

writeControl    timeStep;

runTimeModifiable true;'), FoamfilePydantic(file_name='decomposeParDict', folder_name='system', content='FoamFile

// ************************************************************************* //'), FoamfilePydantic(file_name='fvSolution', folder_name='system', content='FoamFile
{
    version     2.0;
    format      ascii;
    class       dictionary;
    object      fvSolution;
}
// * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * //

solvers
{
    p
    {
        solver          GAMG;
        smoother        GaussSeidel;
        tolerance       1e-06;
        relTol          0.1;
    }

    "(U|h|e|k|epsilon|omega)"
    {
        solver          smoothSolver;
        smoother        symGaussSeidel;
        tolerance       1e-06;
        relTol          0.1;
    }
}

SIMPLE
{
    nNonOrthogonalCorrectors 0;
    pMinFactor      0.1;
    pMaxFactor      1.5;

    residualControl
    {
        p               1e-4;
        U               1e-4;
        "(h|e)"         1e-4;
        k               1e-4;
        epsilon         1e-4;
    }
}

relaxationFactors
{
    fields
    {
        p               0.3;
        rho             0.01;
    }
    equations
    {
        U               0.7;
        "(h|e)"         0.7;
        k               0.7;
        epsilon         0.7;
    }
}

// * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * //

ddtSchemes
{

}

gradSchemes
{
    default         Gauss linear;
    grad(U)         cellLimited Gauss linear 1;
}

divSchemes
{
    default         none;
    div(phi,U)      bounded Gauss linearUpwind grad(U);
    div(phi,e)      bounded Gauss linearUpwind default;
    div(phi,h)      bounded Gauss linearUpwind default;
    div(phi,K)      bounded Gauss linearUpwind default;
    div(phi,k)      bounded Gauss linearUpwind default;
    div(phi,epsilon) bounded Gauss linearUpwind default;
    div(phi,Ekp)    bounded Gauss linearUpwind default;

}

{
    default         Gauss linear corrected;
}

{
    default         linear;
}

snGradSchemes
{
    default         corrected;
}

vertices

    (  0   25  25)  // 9 (shared with Block 0 vertex 7)
    ( 25   25 -25)  // 10
    ( 25   25  25)  // 11
    ( 25  -25 -25)  // 12
    ( 25  -25  25)  // 13
    ( 25 -125 -25)  // 14
    ( 25 -125  25)  // 15

    // Block 2 - outlet straight duct (along -y)
    (-500 -125 -25)  // 16
    (-500 -125  25)  // 17
    (  25 -125 -25)  // 18 (shared with Block 1 vertex 14)
    (  25 -125  25)  // 19 (shared with Block 1 vertex 15)
    (  25  -75 -25)  // 20
    (  25  -75  25)  // 21
);

blocks
(
    // Block 0: Inlet straight duct (along +x)
    hex (0 1 2 3 4 5 6 7) (10 10 10) simpleGrading (1 1 1)

    // Block 1: Bend section
    hex (8 10 11 9 12 13 15 14) (10 10 10) simpleGrading (1 1 1)

    // Block 2: Outlet straight duct (along -y)
    hex (16 18 19 17 20 21 19 17) (10 10 10) simpleGrading (1 1 1)
);

edges
(
);

boundary
(
    inlet
    {
        type patch;
        faces
        (
            (0 4 5 1)
        );
    }

    outlet
    {
        type patch;
        faces
        (
            (16 17 21 20)
        );
    }

    upperWall
    {
        type wall;
        faces
        (
            // Block 0 top
            (4 7 6 5)
            // Block 1 top
            (10 11 15 12)
            // Block 2 top
            (21 19 21 20)
        );
    }

    lowerWall
    {
        type wall;
        faces
        (
            // Block 0 bottom
            (0 3 2 1)
            // Block 1 bottom
            (8 14 15 9)
            // Block 2 bottom
            (16 18 20 17)
        );
    }

    leftWall
    {
        type wall;
        faces
        (
            // Block 0 left (+z side)
            (0 4 7 3)
            // Block 1 left (+z side)
            (8 12 15 14)
            // Block 2 left (+z side)
            (16 20 21 17)
        );
    }

    rightWall

        (
            // Block 0 right (-z side)

            // Block 1 right (-z side)
            (12 13 15 14)
            // Block 2 right (-z side)

        );
    }

    frontWall

);

    in file blockDescriptor/blockDescriptor.C at line 106.

<review_analysis># Error Analysis and Resolution

## Current Errors

Still the same cascade:
1. **`log.blockMesh`**: Multiple blocks have "inward-pointing faces" or are "inside-out"
2. **`log.rhoSimpleFoam`**: Cannot find mesh files (because blockMesh never succeeded)

## Root Cause — The Multi-Block Topology Is Fundamentally Flawed

After 5 attempts, the multi-block approach keeps failing. The fundamental problem is that the **bend region blocks collapse into 2D geometries** because vertices are constrained to only two x-planes (x=0 and x=25 for Block 1, x=-500 and x=25 for Block 2), and the boundary face definitions reference faces that don't correctly map to external surfaces.

Each attempt at reordering vertices or splitting into more blocks hasn't resolved the geometric degeneracy. The blocks simply don't form valid 3D parallelepipeds.

## New Approach — Completely Restructured Mesh Topology

Instead of trying to shoehorn the user's specified vertices into a multi-block topology, **restructure the mesh to use a single hex block with a proper graded mesh** that respects the user's domain extent requirements.

### Key Design Change

Create a **single hex block** with vertices positioned to represent the full domain appropriately:

- **Bottom-left-back (near inlet-outlet connection)**: `(-500, -125, -25)`
- **Top-bottom-back**: `(-500, -125, 25)`
- **Bottom-top-back**: `(-500, 25, -25)`
- **Top-top-back**: `(-500, 25, 25)`
- **Bottom-left-front**: `(-50, -125, -25)`
- **Top-bottom-front**: `(-50, -125, 25)`
- **Bottom-top-front**: `(-50, 25, -25)`
- **Top-top-front**: `(-50, 25, 25)`

This defines a block from x=-500 to x=-50, y=-125 to y=25, z=-25 to z=25. Then add a **second block** extending from x=-50 to x=25 in the y-direction to represent the bend:

- **Block 1 (bend/stem)**: Connects x=-50 to x=25 and y=-125 to y=25
- Actually, restructure as:

**Block 0**: Inlet duct — from x=-50 to x=0, y=-25 to y=25, z=-25 to z=25  
**Block 1**: Bend stem — from x=0 to x=25, y=-125 to y=25, z=-25 to z=25  
**Block 2**: Outlet duct — from x=-500 to x=-50, y=-125 to y=-75, z=-25 to z=25  

No — the critical insight is that **the bend block must span a true 3D volume**. The bend goes from the inlet duct (aligned with x-axis) to the outlet duct (aligned with negative y-axis). So:

**Block 0** (inlet straight): `hex (-50 -25 -25) (0 25 -25) (0 25 -25) (10 10 10)` — no, need 8 vertices.

Let me define proper vertices:

- V0: (-50, -25, -25)  V1: (0, -25, -25)  V2: (0, 25, -25)  V3: (-50, 25, -25)
- V4: (-50, -25, 25)   V5: (0, -25, 25)   V6: (0, 25, 25)    V7: (-50, 25, 25)

That's Block 0: `hex (0 1 2 3 4 5 6 7)` — a proper rectangular prism.

**Block 1** (bend stem): Goes from x=0 to x=25 and y=-125 to y=25. Need 8 new vertices plus shared ones:

- Shared with Block 0 at x=0: V8=(0,-25,-25), V9=(0,25,-25), V10=(0,-25,25), V11=(0,25,25)
- New at x=25: V12=(25,-125,-25), V13=(25,25,-25), V14=(25,-125,25), V15=(25,25,25)

But Block 1 would be `hex (8 12 13 9 10 14 15 11)` — this spans x=0→25 and y=-125→25. Check if valid: yes, it goes from y=-125 to y=25 (different y-faces) and x=0 to x=25 (different x-faces). This should be fine.

**Block 2** (outlet duct): From x=-500 to x=-50, y=-125 to y=-75:

- V16=(-500,-125,-25), V17=(-500,-75,-25), V18=(-500,-125,25), V19=(-500,-75,25)
- V20=(-50,-125,-25), V21=(-50,-75,-25), V22=(-50,-125,25), V23=(-50,-75,25)

Block 2: `hex (16 17 21 18 20 23 22 19)`

Now check boundary faces against actual external faces.

The critical fix compared to all previous attempts: **Block 1 now genuinely spans a 3D volume** (x=0→25, y=-125→25, z=-25→25) with vertices spread across all three dimensions. Previously, Block 1 had all vertices confined to only two x-planes or two y-planes, causing degeneration. This version has vertices at 4 different x-values conceptually (though only 2 x-planes, it spans y and z fully) — actually it spans x={0,25}, y={-125,25}, z={-25,25} — that IS a valid hex.

The real issue in past attempts was always the **boundary face definitions not matching the actual block exteriors**. Every face listed under `inlet`, `outlet`, `upperWall`, etc. must correspond to a face that is on the exterior of exactly one block.

## Recommended Actions

1. **Rewrite `blockMeshDict`** with the three-block topology described above, ensuring:
   - All 24 vertices are uniquely defined
   - Block 0: `hex (0 1 2 3 4 5 6 7)` — inlet duct (x=-50..0, y=-25..25, z=-25..25)
   - Block 1: `hex (8 12 13 9 10 14 15 11)` — bend stem (x=0..25, y=-125..25, z=-25..25)
   - Block 2: `hex (16 17 21 18 20 23 22 19)` — outlet duct (x=-500..-50, y=-125..-75, z=-25..25)
   - Carefully define each boundary face to match ONLY external faces of the assembled blocks

2. **Update all boundary face definitions** (`inlet`, `outlet`, `upperWall`, `lowerWall`, `leftWall`, `rightWall`, `frontWall`, `backWall`) to reference only faces that belong to exactly one block and are on the mesh exterior.

3. **Keep all other files unchanged** — once blockMesh succeeds, rhoSimpleFoam will find the mesh.</review_analysis>
<user_requirement>Conduct a 3D squarebend flow simulation using OpenFOAM. The computational domain includes multiple regions, with vertices defined as inlet points at (-50, 25, 25) and (-50, 25, -25), outlet points at (-500, -75, 25) and (-500, -75, -25), and mid-bend points like (25, 0, 25) and (25, 0, -25). Follow the same geomtery as the tutorial. Use a start time of 0 seconds, an end time of 500 seconds, a time step of 1 second, and write results every 100 seconds. Use a massflow rate inlet of 0.5 with turbulentBL profile. Outlet is pressureInletOutletVelocity. Temperature has an inlet value of 1000.
